problems.problem: Beta testers for Kit/Substack sync tool - 2 min setup
problems.description: In 'the biggest mistake i made as a beginner solopreneur', the author explicitly asks readers to try a new beta tool that syncs Substack subscribers to Kit or other email providers. They're actively seeking users to test it and offering to set people up personally.
evidence: I built a new tool for those of you who use Kit (or any other email provider), to automatically sync new subscribers from Substack to your email provider.
It's currently in beta and you can try it, completely free, no credit card, no commitment.
Reply to the email or send me a DM and I'll set you up.
problems.email_open_reasoning: The author explicitly asks for beta testers and offers personal setup assistance. This is a direct call for help with a new product launch, and they're actively seeking users right now. A stranger offering relevant beta testing expertise or connections would get an immediate response.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is a beta launch with active outreach happening now. The author needs testers immediately, but once the beta period ends and the product stabilizes (typically 1-3 months), this specific need will resolve.
problems.source_post: the biggest mistake i made as a beginner solopreneur

problems.problem: Converting free trial users to paid WriteStack customers
problems.description: The author ran a free weekend promotion for WriteStack that achieved 56 signups but only 5 conversions (8.93%), far below the expected 15-20%. In "i gave writestack away for free heres what happened", they identify specific failures in email sequences, testing, and follow-ups that hurt conversion rates.
evidence: Out of those 56, 5 converted into paid users right after the free period (8.93%). That's where reality hit me. I expected 15–20% conversion. Either I set the bar too high… or I messed up somewhere.
I didn't set up follow up emails after the weekend ended. I didn't even send follow up DMs until today, almost 2 weeks after the event was concluded.
I learned that traffic spikes mean nothing if the funnel leaks. I learned that without clean onboarding, proper follow-ups, and tight promotion, you leave money on the table.
Although I feel like it was a failure, it was more of a validation that people will pay for WriteStack. Just not if you half-ass the marketing.
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is a real operational problem the author is actively working on and has identified specific gaps in (email sequences, follow-ups, onboarding). However, they seem to already know what went wrong and what to fix for next time, suggesting they may not need external strategic advice. A stranger's tactical insights might get read carefully but wouldn't necessarily warrant a reply.
problems.persistence_reasoning: As an ongoing SaaS business, conversion optimization will remain a priority for at least 6 months. The author is clearly planning to run more promotions ("Next time it will be much better!"), so this problem will persist through multiple campaign iterations.
problems.source_post: i gave writestack away for free heres what happened

problems.problem: Promoting WriteStack effectively to reach target users
problems.description: The author acknowledges they "didn't promote it correctly" and "rushed into it just to 'do it'" during the free weekend campaign. In "i gave writestack away for free heres what happened", they admit most traffic came from direct links they personally dropped rather than scalable promotion channels.
evidence: I didn't promote it correctly, I rushed into it just to 'do it'.
Most came from Direct (My notes/links sent).
I learned that without clean onboarding, proper follow-ups, and tight promotion, you leave money on the table.
Just not if you half-ass the marketing.
problems.email_open_reasoning: The author recognizes promotion as a weakness but hasn't specified what kind of help they need or whether they're actively seeking external expertise. This feels like a problem they're aware of but planning to iterate on themselves, making a stranger's cold email about it borderline—they'd read it but might not engage.
problems.persistence_reasoning: Marketing and promotion challenges are structural for indie SaaS products and will persist as long as the author runs WriteStack. Given they're still in growth mode and planning future campaigns, this will be relevant for many months.
problems.source_post: i gave writestack away for free heres what happened
